text: The electrochemical oxidation of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) at the anode is considered as a high-efficient method for biomass-derived aldehyde conversion, producing hydrogen and chemical products simultaneously. The HMF oxidation process occurs at a lower overpotential of ~0.1 V vs. RHE compared to water splitting. So, the reaction mechanism and important intermediates of this process are unclear. Here, the in situ Raman and attenuated total reflectance infrared (ATR-IR) spectroscopy, combined with isotope labeling and density functional theory (DFT) calculations are employed to investigate the oxidation process of HMF on Au-modified Ni foam electrode. Two pH-dependent gem-diol intermediates, namely the 5-(dihydroxymethyl)furan-2-methanol anion (DHMFM$^-$) and dianion (DHMFM$^{2-}$) are identified. The role of these two gem-diol intermediates in the H2 production process at low overpotential is unraveled. The DFT results confirm that the gem-diol dianion acts as a key intermediate, which facilitates the hydride (H$^-$) transfer for producing H2 at a lower overpotential, while the gem-diol monoanion undergoes proton (H$^+$) transfer to produce H2O. The oxidation rate of DHMFM$^-$ increases at a higher electrode potential, which shifts the reaction equilibrium to the side of DHMFM$^{2-}$, thus suppressing and eventually terminating the anodic hydrogen production. Our results clarify the competition of intermediates and identify the important role of the gem-diol dianion, which will guide the construction of efficient electrocatalysts for biomass-derived aldehyde upgrading and hydrogen production.
explanation: The text is highly domain-specific and reads like a credible scientific abstract with concrete experimental details, which suggests human authorship. The inclusion of precise terminology and data-like statements (e.g., specific intermediates, potentials) reduces obvious AI-generation cues.
